summary: Clean architecture is the primary backend architecture frame for separating policy, interface, and infrastructure concerns.
The dependency rule governs clean architecture by requiring dependencies to point inward toward policy.
Dependency inversion is the practical mechanism for correcting outward dependency pressure.
The domain layer is the innermost policy layer in the backend architecture map.
The application layer owns use-case orchestration in the backend architecture map.
The interface layer adapts incoming and outgoing transport interactions.
The infrastructure layer holds persistence and external integration details.
Application use cases depend inward on domain policy and abstractions.
Interface adapters call application use cases rather than bypassing them into infrastructure or domain mechanics.
Infrastructure implements domain-facing abstractions while domain code avoids depending on infrastructure detail.
Repository abstractions implement dependency inversion for persistence.
The repository interface belongs on the domain-facing side of the persistence boundary.
Application services sit in the application layer as use-case coordinators.
A service layer can implement a use-case boundary when it coordinates a business flow without transport or persistence details.
Use cases enter and are coordinated in the application layer.
DTO and schema objects usually sit at interface boundaries where transport data is shaped.
Transaction and session mechanics are infrastructure concerns unless explicitly wrapped behind an application port.
Backend architecture decisions should distinguish system behavior from the structure that enables changing behavior.
Separating structural and behavior changes turns the structure-versus-behavior distinction into a reviewable workflow.
Separating change types reduces the cost of review and rollback.
Coupling increases change cost when one element forces changes or knowledge in another.
Decoupling reduces coupling when the structural work is economically justified.
Cohesion guides where decoupled elements should be grouped so boundaries remain meaningful.
Depending on abstractions reduces concrete coupling between policy code and outer adapters.
Reversible structural changes lower regret and rollback risk under uncertainty.
A reversible structure change still has current cost, so it must be weighed against future option value.
FastAPI is the verified v1 framework adapter for mapping framework features to backend architecture boundaries.
FastAPI routers map to the interface layer because they adapt HTTP requests into application calls.
FastAPI Depends layer leakage is a concrete instance of framework leak risk.
Using FastAPI Depends inside application services risks making application code depend on framework APIs.
The FastAPI adapter includes router/controller mapping.
The FastAPI adapter includes response schema boundary mapping.
FastAPI response models map to the DTO/schema boundary.
SQLAlchemy and Alembic map to infrastructure because they are persistence and migration details.
A concrete SQLAlchemy repository can implement a domain-facing repository abstraction.
Framework leakage is a warning sign that dependency direction may be violated.
A clear use-case boundary separates behavior intent from structural framework and persistence details.
A service layer can coordinate persistence intent while session mechanics remain hidden behind infrastructure or a port.
Explicit DTO/schema boundaries reduce framework and transport data leakage into policy code.
Framework adapter extension is part of the backend architecture skill but remains evidence-gated.
FastAPI is the implemented adapter; other framework adapters should follow this evidence-backed pattern later.
Clean architecture gives structure to keep behavior changes cheaper and more isolated.
Cohesion helps decide what belongs together inside a service or use-case boundary.
Framework leak risk is harmful because it creates coupling between policy code and framework details.
The repository pattern is one backend mechanism for economically justified decoupling.
Depends is part of FastAPI adapter wiring, but it must be placed carefully to avoid layer leakage.
Review and rollback cost explains why the workflow separates structural and behavior changes.
Backend architecture is valuable when it lowers the cost of future changes.
The interface layer includes request and response shaping at DTO/schema boundaries.
The infrastructure layer includes transaction/session boundaries for persistence details.
Spring is a verified framework adapter source for mapping concrete framework features to backend architecture boundaries.
The Spring adapter includes controller mapping for HTTP interface boundaries.
The Spring adapter includes service component mapping for application coordination.
The Spring adapter includes repository mapping for persistence boundaries.
The Spring adapter includes JPA persistence mapping for infrastructure concerns.
The Spring adapter includes OpenAPI design-first and generated-contract boundary mapping.
The Spring adapter includes WebFlux and reactive persistence boundary mapping.
The Spring adapter includes Spring Security and JWT boundary mapping.
Spring REST controllers map to the interface layer because they adapt HTTP requests into application calls.
Spring services map to the application layer when they coordinate use cases and keep framework mechanics outside policy code.
A Spring service component can implement the service-layer pattern when it owns orchestration rather than transport or database details.
Spring repositories map to the repository pattern as a persistence boundary around data access.
JPA persistence maps to infrastructure because ORM and database configuration are concrete technical details.
A JPA-backed repository can implement the repository pattern behind an application-facing persistence boundary.
OpenAPI specifications and generated interfaces map to DTO/schema boundary concerns for request and response contracts.
Design-first API contracts support interface adapters by stabilizing endpoint shapes before implementation.
WebFlux maps to interface adapter concerns when reactive controllers or functional endpoints adapt HTTP requests.
Reactive persistence such as R2DBC supports non-blocking infrastructure boundaries when the pipeline must remain reactive.
Reactive type leakage is a concrete Spring instance of framework leak risk.
Exposing Mono or Flux through application contracts can couple application code to WebFlux/Reactor choices.
Spring Security filters and bearer-token handling sit before or around interface adapters rather than inside domain policy.
Security context leakage is a concrete Spring instance of framework leak risk.
Application services should receive stable identity or authorization decisions instead of depending on Spring Security context mechanics.
FastAPI and Spring adapters can be compared as framework-specific mappings onto the same architecture boundaries.
API contracts map to interface boundaries because they define how external callers interact with backend behavior.
API design supports DTO and schema boundaries by making request, response, authentication, and authorization contracts explicit.
Data modeling decisions connect to infrastructure because storage engines, schemas, indexes, and transactions are concrete persistence concerns.
Transaction and consistency decisions are part of the data modeling boundary.
The data layer supports repository-style encapsulation of persistence access and domain-facing data operations.
ORM, migration, and concrete data-access mechanics belong in infrastructure-facing data-layer code.
Queues and asynchronous events can decouple long-running work from request-response flows.
Event-driven decoupling requires operational readiness for state tracking, retries, priority, scheduling, and failure handling.
Monolith and microservice choices trade change isolation against communication, deployment, and operational cost.
Service boundaries should follow cohesive change and ownership areas rather than technology preference alone.
Configuration, deployment, runtime shape, monitoring, and failure response are part of backend architecture quality.
Runtime process structure, deployment mechanics, and operational configuration often live at infrastructure boundaries.
Tests and TDD make small architecture changes more reversible by providing fast behavioral feedback.
A strong test feedback loop lowers review and rollback risk for backend structural changes.
Packages make cohesive reusable boundaries explicit through distribution, versioning, and environment isolation.
A well-formed package boundary can decouple independently reusable backend components.
Logs, metrics, profiling, and debugging evidence improve operational readiness and failure response.
Runtime evidence increases the quality of architecture evolution decisions by replacing guesses with observations.
Continuous architecture applies the structure-versus-behavior distinction as incremental evolution instead of broad rewrites.
Incremental architecture evolution favors reversible steps under uncertainty.
Better runtime evidence can reduce the cost of choosing and validating backend changes.
DDD strategic design adds business-value and language evidence to backend architecture boundary decisions.
Strategic design starts by distinguishing core, supporting, and generic subdomains.
Core subdomains are stronger candidates for rich domain models when behavior and invariants justify the cost.
Supporting or generic subdomains often justify simpler implementation styles instead of expensive rich modeling.
Simple patterns reduce current implementation cost when domain behavior is straightforward and low differentiation.
Ubiquitous language is reliable inside the bounded context where terms carry consistent model meaning.
A bounded context groups language, model, and ownership around cohesive business meaning.
Bounded contexts inform service decomposition, but operational tradeoffs still decide whether they become separate deployable services.
Context mapping makes bounded contexts and their relationships visible for planning and integration decisions.
Context maps reveal where anticorruption layers are needed to protect local models from upstream language or data shape.
The same boundary instinct that prevents framework leaks also protects local models from external model leakage.
DTO/schema boundaries can carry translation logic when protecting a local model from an external integration model.
Aggregates map to transaction boundaries because invariants should be enforced in intentionally small consistency scopes.
Repositories should usually load and save aggregates instead of exposing arbitrary persistence internals.
Entities, value objects, aggregates, and domain events belong in policy-facing domain code when behavior is complex enough.
Domain events make event-driven integration safer when event meaning is grounded in a model boundary.
Event sourcing and CQRS extend event-driven modeling when history and read/write model divergence justify the complexity.
Event sourcing and CQRS add audit and query-model power but raise migration, storage, projection, and operational complexity.
Event sourcing and CQRS are usually excessive for simple procedural domains where history is not a core model concern.
Event storming validates candidate context boundaries by exposing events, commands, policies, and language shifts.
Event storming can surface candidate aggregates after commands, policies, and invariants become visible.
Data mesh extends domain boundary and language thinking into analytical data ownership and data product design.
Useful domain-owned data products need language that operational and analytical consumers interpret consistently.
Microservice slicing should follow strategic domain understanding rather than technology fashion or every candidate model boundary.
Modern Java idioms support Spring service readability when they clarify orchestration without hiding transport, persistence, or business policy boundaries.
Optional can document absence near lookup or API boundaries, but DTO and schema contracts still need explicit project conventions.
Stream pipelines should not hide business side effects or shared state mutation inside application flow code.
CompletableFuture can coordinate asynchronous backend calls when async ownership and failure handling are explicit at the boundary.
Async composition improves latency options but raises executor, timeout, cancellation, and error-path responsibilities.
Runtime concerns support framework adapters but should not leak inward into policy or use-case decisions.
API contracts are more useful when security risk and observability evidence are modeled as explicit boundaries.
Reversible structure changes and option value refine the broader change-cost economics used by backend architecture decisions.
public-safe knowledge boundary는 coverage workflow가 원문 대체물로 흐르지 않도록 제약한다.
coverage matrix는 query QA가 어떤 source slice와 topic을 검증해야 하는지 범위를 제공한다.
source quality lane은 needs_review source를 summary-only limited lane으로 보내는 기준이다.
검수 완료 source는 normal lane에서 더 넓은 concept coverage를 허용한다.
curated topic index는 유용한 탐색성을 제공하지만 누적되면 market substitute risk를 감시해야 한다.
canonical registry는 exactMatch보다 약한 closeMatch를 별도 관계로 관리한다.
canonical registry는 architecture 개념과 framework 구현 대응을 maps_to로 분리한다.
adapter evidence rule은 framework adapter extension을 source graph 근거가 있을 때만 허용한다.
independent source promotion은 backend meta node 승격의 증거 수 기준을 정의한다.
expression leakage stop gate는 public-safe boundary를 위반하는 직접 표현 복제를 차단한다.
graph density sufficiency는 단순 edge 수가 아니라 query QA로 쓰이는 의미 연결을 요구한다.
stable source ref 정책은 backend meta skill이 public graph ID만 사용하게 한다.
manual sample review는 자동 verbatim gate가 놓칠 수 있는 표현 유사성을 보완한다.
market substitute risk와 heart-of-work risk는 양과 핵심성 측면의 public-safety 판단을 함께 다룬다.
batch rollback unit은 sample review 실패 시 해당 skill reference batch만 되돌리게 한다.
public graph only meta skill은 backend-architecture의 source synthesis 정책에 속한다.
